name: Thattai
description: Thattai, also known as Thattai Murukku or Thattu Vadai, is a crispy, crunchy South Indian deep-fried snack made from rice flour dough flattened into thin discs. Flavoured with soaked chana dal, urad dal, sesame seeds and curry leaves, these flat crackers are a festival favourite during Diwali and Gokulashtami. Each thattai is individually pressed and fried to golden perfection.

steps.instruction: Soak chana dal and urad dal in water for 1 hour. Drain and set aside.
Dry roast rice flour for 2 minutes on medium flame without changing colour. Sieve into a mixing bowl.
Add urad dal flour, fried gram dal flour, soaked chana dal, sesame seeds, red chilli powder, asafoetida, chopped curry leaves, salt and softened butter.
Mix everything well first. Then add water little by little to form a smooth, soft dough.
steps.tip: The dough should be slightly loose, not too dry, or it will crack while pressing.

steps.instruction: Pinch small portions and roll into gooseberry-sized balls. Keep covered to prevent drying.
Grease a butter paper or zip-lock cover with oil. Place a ball and fold the sheet over.
Using a flat-bottomed bowl or your fingers, press gently into a thin, even disc.
steps.tip: Press the thattai as thin and even as possible — the thinner, the crispier.

steps.instruction: Prick the disc with a fork to prevent puffing while frying.
Heat oil on medium heat. Carefully slide the thattai into the hot oil.
steps.tip: Oil temperature is crucial — too low and thattai will be soggy; too high and it will brown too quickly.

steps.instruction: Fry 3 to 4 at a time on medium-low flame. Flip and fry until golden on both sides.
steps.tip: Do not make too many layers in the oil — 2 circles per thattai is enough.

steps.instruction: Continue frying until the bubbling sound completely ceases, then cook 1 more minute for extra crispiness.
Drain on paper towels. Cool and store in an airtight container.
steps.tip: Thattai stays crisp for 2 to 3 weeks in an airtight container.

tips: Press the thattai as thin and even as possible — the thinner, the crispier.
The dough should be slightly loose, not too dry, or it will crack while pressing.
Chop curry leaves very finely so they don't interrupt while pressing.
Oil temperature is crucial — too low and thattai will be soggy; too high and it will brown too quickly.
Do not make too many layers in the oil — 2 circles per thattai is enough.
You can add roasted peanuts for extra crunch and flavour.
Thattai stays crisp for 2 to 3 weeks in an airtight container.
